checks.a_analysis: The output includes a heading 'Meeting Agenda' which is not explicitly present in the source text. However, the source uses Roman numerals (I, II, III, IV) to denote agenda items. Per instructions, section labels added by the pipeline for structural organization are acceptable and not considered fabrications. No other invented body text was found.
The source document contains no phone numbers, email addresses, mailing addresses, or URLs. The output correctly reflects this absence.

All primary content sections of the minutes (Header, Attendees, Absent members, and the minutes of the meeting items I through IV) are present in the output. The omission of specific metadata text (footer/stamp) is addressed under C5.
The source document contains columns of names that were appropriately converted into list structures. There are no formal tables in the document to evaluate for row count.

This omission is significant as it removes the only record of the document's official approval date (August 23, 2019) from both the body text and the metadata.

checks.a_analysis: The text content that was successfully extracted matches the source accurately, including the preservation of a typographical error ('Chariman' instead of 'Chairman' in section I). No meaning-altering discrepancies were found in the captured text.

checks.a_analysis: The output JSON does not contain any duplicated content. Each section, list item, and paragraph from the source document is represented exactly once.

The document starts with H2 levels for 'MINUTES' and 'NORTH CAROLINA 911 BOARD MEETING', followed by H3 levels for the date, call type, and time. Subsections are used correctly for organizational labels like 'Members Present via Teleconference'. This hierarchy is logical and skips no levels.

The source document presents attendee lists in two columns. The JSON converts these into titled subsections with lists rather than a formal table structure. This is an acceptable structural interpretation for side-by-side text columns that are not semantically a data table.
The reading order in the JSON follows the top-to-bottom, left-to-right flow of the source document, starting with header info, then attendee lists, then the numbered meeting minutes.

There are several minor structural issues with list conversion. 1) The attendee lists (Members and Staff) are rendered as single list items containing a block of text with newlines, rather than individual items per person. 2) The main agenda list (I-IV) is split into two separate ordered list elements separated by a paragraph. 3) The paragraph starting with 'Mr. Greene made the motion...' is visually and logically part of item III in the source, but is extracted as a standalone paragraph in the JSON, breaking the continuity of item III. 4) The last list item includes the hardcoded label 'IV.' within the text of an ordered list item, which is structurally redundant.
checks.b_evidence_source: Item III. Senate Bill 600... [includes paragraph] Mr. Greene made the motion...
checks.b_evidence_output: The JSON ends the first list after the first paragraph of Item III, inserts a standalone paragraph, then starts a new list for Item IV.

checks.a_analysis: There are no links in the source document, and none were injected into the JSON.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output faithfully translates the PDF's layout into a responsive web format. The two-column arrangement of attendee and staff lists in the source is transformed into a single-column sequential layout, which is standard for accessible HTML. The addition of a navigation sidebar (Contents) and the reorganization of header information into distinct headings are acceptable and expected improvements. The visual flow of information—from the meeting metadata to the attendee lists and finally the agenda items—remains logically consistent with the source document.

The source document does not contain formal data tables. The multi-column layout of the attendee lists is effectively represented as standard bulleted lists in the HTML output, ensuring the content remains readable and organized.

The meaningful logos (NC 911 Board / DIT) found at the top and bottom of the source PDF are correctly rendered in the HTML output. The red diagonal watermark stamp is omitted, which aligns with the instructions to hide decorative or background elements as an accessibility improvement.

No body content duplication was observed. Although the document's header information and section titles are repeated in the table of contents sidebar, this is a permitted accessible design feature. The core text, lists, and agenda details appear exactly once in the main body of the document.
